item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
1. Similarities between SCP-2955 and SCP-2583 have been noted. Investigation into a potential shared origin is ongoing.

While I agree that the similarities noted are within reason, I do not necessarily agree with implying a shared origin (whether if it can be applicable or not).

Thus, my proposed solution to this is:

1. Similarities between SCP-2955 and SCP-2583 have been noted. [DATA REDACTED]

This might seem to be a cop-out, but I think it would leave room for readers to fill in the blank, I hope.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Okay! So when I was reading through this article, I was reminded of SCP-2583. Both articles feature living particulate organisms, and its not so far out of the realm of possibility for the SCPF to investigate a shared origin. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2955 refers to a collection of granular particles, capable of autonomous movement. An exact total number of SCP-2955 instances cannot be verified, partly due to their anomalous properties. Current estimations suggest there are at least 4,000,000 such instances.

To this:

SCP-2955 refers to a collection of granular particles, capable of autonomous movement. An exact total number of SCP-2955 instances cannot be verified, partly due to their anomalous properties. Current estimations suggest there are at least 4,000,000 such instances.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarities between SCP-2955 and SCP-2583 have been noted. Investigation into a potential shared origin is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote, could use some tense cleanup in the last logs, PM me when that's done so I can come back and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… if we look at the SCP map, I would argue that most anomalies tend to cluster along the US East Coast instead.

And yes, it is "Every Wandering…". It offers the implication that every city of gold known in South American legends refer to the same thing. I won't call it "Ever Wandering…" since it only started wandering when Conquistadors tore down other members of its kind for spare gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one. I find it interesting that many of the larger SCPs tend to cluster in South America. Guess they're the Texas of Anomalies?

One question: Is the mainlist title supposed to be "Every Wandering City" or "Ever Wandering City?"

Either way, +1 for the lost city(ies) of gold!

Edit: I meant the larger (as in size, not density) items tend to be on or near the southern continent (SCP-455, 169, 1986). Maybe it's just the order I read them in, but I see a subjective trend towards massive sites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ironically, the context of said SCP is out of mythology and folklore. Prefacing it with a scientific outlook would be, in my opinion, how the Foundation would perceive it and treat the subject-matter as such.

The Foundation is technically a scientific organisation, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well it's more a personal preference, but I'm not a fan of science-y SCPs, as I have a hard time understanding STEM stuff.

Like:

SCP-2955 refers to a collection of granular particles,1 capable of autonomous movement. Individual instances of SCP-2955 have a measured diameter of 15 mm on average. Samples taken indicate that SCP-2955 is composed of a mixture of tumbaga2 and silica.

This sort of put me to sleep.

Again, this is a personal preference and I'm not suggesting you change it, but this kind of Scip just isn't for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I like everything about this, but especially that it graduated from the Events page. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept, good follow-through. Not amazing, but it's okay. No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see… Which aspect of the article ultimately sent your interest crashing down?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Competently written from a technical standpoint, but my eyes glazed over while reading it and it failed to keep my interest. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It's a unique and creative way to look at the myths of golden cities (which were, conveniently, always "Just over that other hill… Far away from our tribe over by those jerks we don't like!"). A nice, solid SCP that keeps to itself and simply does what it does. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't add a nickname for the SCP on the mainlist yet. I will get to that when I pass the "12-hour test".1

+ show block
– hide block


As for the first question, I would say that the in-universe Foundation want to conceal the implications of the act. Said implication is that SCP-2955 might be sapient (as it somehow acknowledged the return of the lost pieces and showed some level of gratitude for the act), although it is hinted at the other addendum.

On the lower levels, it might be passed off as a "sentient gold" type of animal. On the higher levels, it would reveal the anomaly to be a race of "sentient gold" that actively interacted with humans (by providing native humans gold in exchange for providing them living space).

EDIT: Okay, you got a point and I've altered the last addendum as a result.

Footnotes
1. Something I coined to determine if an article is well-received on this site. A pass (more or less) is indicated when the article's rating >10 at 12 hours after posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a minor thing, and i may just be ignorant, but why does the part about gold nuggets and molting require level 3 clearance? Why not just an addendum?

Also your page still says access denied instead of the title on the mainlist at time of writing.

item.title: Thanks, Trivia, Explanation, and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, allow me to thank all who had reviewed: Cimmerian, DreamwalkerFae, Leviathan34, Illustrio, NonPlusUltra and anyone whom I somehow missed out.

+ show block
– hide block

To clarify, this is not El Dorado. El Dorado (the golden one) refers to a tribal chief who is said to cover himself in gold dust and dive into a lake.

This, however, is the following:

  • Seven Cities of Gold
  • Cibola and Quivira
  • City of the Caesars
  • The golden city that was mislabelled as "El Dorado"
  • And every single "lost city of gold" in Latin America

As to what is happening, SCP-2955 routinely "moult" gold nuggets which adds a surplus to Latin American gold. The Conquistadors decided to just grab everything that is made of gold, and SCP-2955 made a desperate attempt to escape them, hence its anomalous effect is simply a very drastic defence mechanism.

The last portion of the article was SCP-2955 deciding that the Foundation ultimately are "good people" like the natives were (as the Foundation opted to return the sample they took), in contrast to the "bad people" (i.e. the Conquistadors).
